section.title: Stand in Awe of God
section.sectionRationale: Amid a book that keeps finding vapor everywhere, the Teacher turns to the one place where solid ground is found: the presence of God. He counsels reverence in worship, restraint in speech, and faithfulness in vows, because God is in heaven and we are on earth. The passage answers the whole book's ache with a single steadying command that will become its conclusion, fear God. It is the still center of Ecclesiastes, where the futility of empty words meets the weight of the living God.
section.thesis: Because God is in heaven and we are on earth, we are to draw near to listen rather than to babble, to keep our words few and our vows faithful, and above all to fear God, the one anchor in a world of vapor.
section.frameNote: Two movements: guard your steps, approaching God to listen with few words rather than the sacrifice of fools; and fulfill your vows, keeping faith with what you promise God, closing on the summons that anchors the book, fear God.

verses.text: Guard your steps when you go to the house of {{elohim|God}}. Draw near to listen rather than to offer the sacrifice of fools, who do not know that they do wrong.
Do not be quick to speak, and do not be hasty in your heart to utter a word before {{elohim|God}}. For God is in heaven and you are on earth. So let your words be few.
As a dream comes through many cares, so the speech of a fool comes with many words.
When you make a vow to God, do not delay in fulfilling it, because He takes no pleasure in fools. Fulfill your vow.
It is better not to vow than to make a vow and not fulfill it.
Do not let your mouth cause your flesh to sin, and do not tell the messenger that your vow was a mistake. Why should God be angry with your words and destroy the work of your hands?
For as many dreams bring {{hevel|futility}}, so do many words. Therefore, fear {{elohim|God}}.

elohim.gloss: God, the Deity. Ecclesiastes always names God as Elohim, the sovereign Creator who is in heaven while we are on earth. In this passage He is the God whose house we enter with guarded steps, before whom our words must be few, and whom we are finally commanded to fear.

hevel.gloss: Vapor, breath, mist. The keyword of the book, rendered futility, meaningless, or fleeting. Here the many words of the fool, like the tangle of many dreams, are hevel, empty breath that vanishes, set against the solid weight of fearing God.

basic.context: After chapters of finding vapor in pleasure, work, wealth, and power, the Teacher turns to worship, the one place where we meet solid ground. He warns us to watch our steps as we come before God: come to listen, not to rattle off the empty religious talk of fools. Because God is in heaven and we are on earth, our words should be few. He then presses the point with vows: if you promise God something, keep it; better to make no vow than to break one. Careless words before God are dangerous. The whole passage lands on one command that becomes the message of the book, fear God. In a world where so much is fleeting, reverence for God is the anchor.
basic.hermeneutics: The key phrase is God is in heaven and you are on earth, which sets the tone: awe, not casualness. The fear of God here is not cringing terror but covenant reverence, the awed, trusting respect owed to the Creator by the creature. The warnings about many words and rash vows are not against prayer or commitment, but against treating God lightly, as if He were an equal to be managed with talk. The passage is the still, positive center that the rest of the book's darkness has been driving toward.
basic.originalLanguages: God is named Elohim, the Creator who is in heaven while we are on earth, and the passage circles from His house, verse 1, to His anger, verse 6, to the command to fear Him, verse 7. Against Him stands hevel, vapor: the many words of the fool are as empty and fleeting as a crowd of dreams. The contrast is the point, empty breath on one side, the weighty reality of God on the other, and the reader is told which to reckon with.
basic.typology: Drawing near to listen rather than to speak points ahead to the greater Word, the Son through whom God has finally spoken, and to whom we are told to listen. The care about vows and truthful speech before God anticipates Christ, who kept perfect faith with the Father and taught let your yes be yes. And the command to fear God is fulfilled in the Son who, in the days of His flesh, was heard because of His reverent submission.
basic.christ: The passage tells us to draw near and listen, and the New Testament says God has spoken finally in His Son, listen to Him. Where our words are vapor and our vows so easily broken, Christ is the faithful and true witness who kept every promise and never sinned with His lips. Through Him we draw near to God with confidence rather than dread, our fear of God deepened into the reverent love of children who have been welcomed home. He is the one whose reverent obedience was fully pleasing to the Father.
basic.god: God is in heaven, exalted and sovereign, not to be handled with careless words or empty ritual. He is holy, so that rash vows and foolish talk before Him are perilous. Yet He is the God who invites us into His house to listen and to draw near, and the fear He commands is the doorway to wisdom and life, not a barrier to it. He takes no pleasure in fools but delights in reverent, faithful worshipers.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 66 says God dwells with the humble and contrite who tremble at His word. Matthew 6 warns against heaping up empty phrases in prayer. James 5 echoes let your yes be yes. Hebrews 12 calls us to worship with reverence and awe, for our God is a consuming fire.
basic.oneStory: In a book that keeps exposing life under the sun as vapor, chapter 5 opens the one door to solid ground: the fear of God. Reverence, restraint, and faithful speech mark the creature who knows that God is in heaven. This is the anchor the whole book has been reaching for, and it finds its fullness in Christ, through whom we draw near to God and learn to fear Him as beloved children.

context.literary: This short unit shifts from the observational I saw of the surrounding chapters to direct commands: guard your steps, do not be quick to speak, fulfill your vow, fear God. It is framed by the setting of worship, the house of God in verse 1, and closes on the imperative that will become the book's conclusion in chapter 12. The proverb of verse 3 and the summary of verse 7 use the image of dreams and many words to expose empty religiosity.
context.historical: The Teacher writes to worshipers who could treat the temple casually, offering sacrifices and making vows while hearts were far off, the same hollow religion the prophets denounced. Vows were solemn, often binding promises made to God, sometimes in a moment of need, and breaking them was no light matter in Israel's covenant life.
context.canonical: The passage stands with the prophets' insistence that God desires obedience and a listening heart over empty sacrifice, and with the wisdom tradition that names the fear of the LORD as the beginning of wisdom. Its counsel on few words and kept vows is taken up by Jesus in the Sermon on the Mount and by James, and its call to reverent worship echoes into Hebrews.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To call the worshiper to reverence before the God who is in heaven, guarding the approach, restraining the tongue, keeping faith with vows, and above all fearing God, so that worship is solid reality rather than the vapor of empty words.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Unlike much of the book, this passage is largely prescriptive: guard your steps, draw near to listen, let your words be few, fulfill your vows, fear God. The one description, verse 3, that many words mark a fool as surely as many cares breed dreams, supports the commands rather than merely observing life.

debates.question: Is fear God, 5:7, mere caution before a dangerous deity, or covenant reverence?

views.label: Prudent caution
views.case: The surrounding warnings, that God may be angry and destroy the work of your hands, verse 6, can make fear God sound like careful self protection before a powerful being who must not be provoked.

views.label: Covenant reverence
views.case: Across Ecclesiastes and the wider canon the fear of God is the awed, trusting respect of the creature for the Creator, the beginning of wisdom and the whole duty of man, chapter 12, a posture of worship and obedience rather than mere dread.

debates.bearing: Read as covenant reverence, fear God is not cringing before a threat but the settled awe that anchors a life; it is the positive answer the whole book has been driving toward, and in Christ it matures into the reverent love of adopted children.

debates.question: What is the danger the passage warns against in worship?

views.label: Empty, careless words
views.case: The repeated stress on few words, the sacrifice of fools, and the folly of many words points to thoughtless, hollow religious talk offered without a listening heart.

views.label: Rash and broken vows
views.case: Verses 4 to 6 focus on promises made to God and then delayed or excused away, treating solemn commitments to God as if they could be shrugged off.

debates.bearing: Both dangers are one at root, treating God lightly; the remedy is the same reverence that listens before it speaks and keeps faith with what it promises.

study.originalLanguages: God is Elohim throughout, the Creator who is in heaven while we are on earth, and the passage moves from His house to His anger to the command to fear Him. The counterweight is hevel, vapor: the many words of the fool, like a swarm of dreams born of many cares, are empty breath that comes to nothing. The Teacher sets the two side by side so the choice is plain, the fleeting babble of the fool, or reverent, listening, faithful worship before the weighty reality of God.

typology.type: Draw near to listen rather than to speak, 5:1-2
typology.fulfillment: The Father's word at the mount, this is My beloved Son, listen to Him, and the Son through whom God has finally spoken, Hebrews 1:1-2.

typology.type: Keeping faith with vows and truthful speech, 5:4-6
typology.fulfillment: Christ the faithful and true witness who never sinned with His lips and taught let your yes be yes, Matthew 5:37.
The Son whose reverent submission was heard by the Father, and in whom our fear becomes the reverent love of children, Hebrews 5:7.

study.christ: The passage tells us to draw near and to listen, and the gospel announces that God has now spoken fully in His Son, whom we are commanded to hear. Our own words are vapor and our vows fragile, but Christ is the faithful and true witness who kept perfect faith with the Father and never sinned in speech. Through Him the barrier of God is in heaven and you are on earth is bridged: we draw near with confidence, and the fear the Teacher commands matures into the reverent love of those adopted as children, who tremble at God's word and delight in it at once.
study.god: God is Elohim in heaven, high and sovereign, not to be managed by many words or empty offerings. He is holy, taking no pleasure in fools, and rightly angered by careless vows. Yet He opens His house and bids us draw near to listen, and the fear He commands is the beginning of wisdom and the anchor of a whole life. He is the weighty reality against which every vapor is measured, and to reverence Him is to stand on solid ground.

crossReferences.note: Heaven is God's throne and earth His footstool, yet He looks to the humble and contrite who tremble at His word.
Do not heap up empty phrases in prayer, thinking you will be heard for many words, an echo of let your words be few.
Jesus on vows and truthful speech, let your yes be yes and your no be no.
Worship God with reverence and awe, for our God is a consuming fire, the New Testament shape of fear God.

study.oneStory: Chapter 5 opens the one door in Ecclesiastes that leads off the treadmill of vapor: the fear of God. Reverence before the God who is in heaven, restraint of the tongue, and faithful vows mark the creature who knows his place before the Creator. This is the anchor the book has been straining toward and will name as its conclusion, and it is fulfilled in Christ, through whom we draw near to listen, are kept faithful, and learn to fear God as beloved children on solid ground.

quiz.q: What posture does the Teacher urge for coming to the house of God, 5:1?

options.text: Guarded steps and a readiness to listen, rather than the empty offering of fools.
options.note: Worship begins with reverence and a listening heart, not careless talk.

options.text: A humble and attentive approach to God.
options.note: True, though the verse specifically contrasts listening with the sacrifice of fools.

options.text: Bringing the most impressive sacrifice you can afford.
options.note: The verse values a listening heart over the mere act of sacrifice.

options.text: Approaching God however feels natural in the moment.
options.note: The whole point is to guard your steps rather than come casually.

quiz.q: Why does the Teacher say to let your words be few, 5:2?

options.text: Because God is in heaven and you are on earth, a gap that calls for reverence.
options.note: The Creator creature distance is the reason for restraint before God.

options.text: Because we should be humble and thoughtful when we speak to God.
options.note: True, and the verse grounds this in God being in heaven while we are on earth.

options.text: Because God does not want to hear from us very often.
options.note: God invites us to draw near; the point is reverence, not distance.

options.text: Because prayer does not really matter to God.
options.note: The passage assumes real worship; it warns against careless words, not against prayer.

quiz.q: What does the Teacher teach about vows in 5:4-6?

options.text: Keep them without delay; better not to vow than to make a vow and break it.
options.note: Faithfulness to what we promise God is the heart of these verses.

options.text: Be careful about the promises you make to God.
options.note: True, but the specific command is to fulfill the vow rather than excuse it away.

options.text: Vows to God can be undone by explaining they were a mistake.
options.note: Verse 6 warns against exactly this, calling the mouth's words to account.

options.text: It does not matter whether you keep a vow or not.
options.note: The passage treats vows with great seriousness before God.

quiz.q: What single command does the passage land on in 5:7?

options.text: Fear God, the anchor set against the vapor of many words and dreams.
options.note: Fear God is the summit here and becomes the book's conclusion.

options.text: Avoid speaking too much, since many words are empty.
options.note: True to the verse's first half, but its climax is the command to fear God.

options.text: Pay attention to your dreams as messages from God.
options.note: The verse treats many dreams as vapor, not as revelation to heed.

options.text: Make as many vows as you can to please God.
options.note: The passage counsels caution with vows, not multiplying them.

quiz.q: What does fear God mean in this passage?

options.text: Covenant reverence, the awed and trusting respect of the creature for the Creator.
options.note: The fear of God is the beginning of wisdom, not cringing dread.

options.text: A serious, respectful caution in how we approach God.
options.note: Close, though biblical fear is fuller, a worshiping reverence, not mere caution.

options.text: Being afraid that God is waiting to punish us.
options.note: The fear commanded is reverent awe, which in Christ becomes the love of children.

options.text: A vague feeling of religious guilt.
options.note: Fear of God is a settled, worshiping reverence, not free floating guilt.

questions: The Teacher says to draw near to listen rather than to rush into speech. When you come to God, are you mostly talking or mostly listening, and what might change if you listened more?
God is in heaven and you are on earth. How would a fresh sense of that distance reshape the way you pray and worship this week?
He warns against vows made lightly and broken easily. Is there a promise you have made to God that you have quietly let slide, and what would faithfulness look like now?
Many words, like many dreams, can be vapor. Where has your religious talk outrun your actual listening and obedience?
Fear God is the anchor the whole book reaches for. What would it mean for reverent awe of God to steady you in a season where so much else feels fleeting?

translationNotes.note: Futility translates hevel, literally vapor or breath, the book's keyword; the fool's many words, like a tangle of dreams, are as empty and fleeting as breath.
God here, as throughout Ecclesiastes, is Elohim, the sovereign Creator; the phrase in 5:2, God is in heaven and you are on earth, sets the tone of reverence for the whole passage.
The messenger can also be rendered angel, as the footnote of the source notes; either way the point is that excusing a broken vow to God's representative does not undo the offense.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Ecclesiastes 5:1-7. Poetic lines rendered as flowing prose within each verse. All em and en dashes converted to commas; no em or en dashes used.
